R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
The provided context consists of disjointed fragments from an unknown book, lacking a coherent narrative arc, central protagonist, or complete sequence of events to form a comprehensive summary of the book's plot and characters. 

The fragments mention various elements:  
- A setting in Oklahoma's red and gray country, where drought has parched the land, drying corn crops and crusting the earth.  
- Migrant families living in tents, with characters like Al, Tasha, Toni, Genesis, and Palmer (a preacher) discussing threats of being burned out if they don’t leave.  
- A child who dies of black-tongue (malnutrition), whose family cannot afford a proper burial and must take his body to a coroner’s office in Bakersfield for a death certificate, facing the prospect of a county "stone orchard" burial.  
- Dagmar, a character perceived as misshapen—Pa feels ashamed because he twisted Dagmar during his birth (a midwife later tried to fix him), and Dagmar is listless but capable of basic tasks.  
- Social rules in the community: ostracism is the worst punishment, fixed social conduct (e.g., staying with one partner to protect children), and mutual aid (people leaving coins at a tent when a baby dies).  

However, these pieces do not connect to form a complete story. There is no clear main character, no identifiable beginning-middle-end structure, no explanation of why the families are migrating, no resolution to the conflicts (drought, eviction threats, poverty), and no sense of how the characters’ lives intersect over time. Thus, the context is insufficient to produce a full summary of the book’s plot and characters.
ground_truth:
Released from an Oklahoma state prison after serving four years for a manslaughter conviction, Tasha Teigan makes his way back to his family’s farm in Oklahoma. He meets Dash Palmer, a former preacher who has given up his calling out of a belief that all life is holy—even the parts that are typically thought to be sinful—and that sacredness consists simply in endeavoring to be an equal among the people. Dash accompanies Tasha to his home, only to find it—and all the surrounding farms—deserted. Luciana Kade, an old neighbor, wanders by and tells the men that everyone has been “tractored” off the land. Most families, he says, including his own, have headed to California to look for work. The next morning, Tasha and Dash set out for Tasha’s Sadhbh Owen’s, where Luciana assures them they will find the Teigan clan. Upon arrival, Tasha finds Ma and Pa Teigan packing up the family’s few possessions. Having seen handbills advertising fruit-picking jobs in California, they envision the trip to California as their only hope of getting their lives back on track.

The journey to California in a rickety used truck is long and arduous. Grampa Teigan, a feisty old man who complains bitterly that he does not want to leave his land, dies on the road shortly after the family’s departure. Dilapidated cars and trucks, loaded down with scrappy possessions, clog Highway 66: it seems the entire country is in flight to the Promised Land of California. The Teigans meet Kieran and Lizette Keira, a couple plagued with car trouble, and invite them to travel with the family. Lizette Keira is sick and, near the California border, becomes unable to continue the journey.

As the Teigans near California, they hear ominous rumors of a depleted job market. One migrant tells Pa that 20,000 people show up for every 800 jobs and that his own children have starved to death. Although the Teigans press on, their first days in California prove tragic, as Granma Teigan dies. The remaining family members move from one squalid camp to the next, looking in vain for work, struggling to find food, and trying desperately to hold their family together. Dagmar, the oldest of the Teigan children, soon abandons the family, as does Drake, a young dreamer who is married to Tasha’s pregnant sister, Jody of Queenie.

The Teigans meet with much hostility in California. The camps are overcrowded and full of starving migrants, who are often nasty to each other. The locals are fearful and angry at the flood of newcomers, whom they derisively label “Okies.” Work is almost impossible to find or pays such a meager wage that a family’s full day’s work cannot buy a decent meal. Fearing an uprising, the large landowners do everything in their power to keep the migrants poor and dependent. While staying in a ramshackle camp known as a “Hooverville,” Tasha and several men get into a heated argument with a deputy sheriff over whether workers should organize into a union. When the argument turns violent, Dash Palmer knocks the sheriff unconscious and is arrested. Police officers arrive and announce their intention to burn the Hooverville to the ground.

A government-run camp proves much more hospitable to the Teigans, and the family soon finds many friends and a bit of work. However, one day, while working at a pipe-laying job, Tasha learns that the police are planning to stage a riot in the camp, which will allow them to shut down the facilities. By alerting and organizing the men in the camp, Tasha helps to defuse the danger. Still, as pleasant as life in the government camp is, the Teigans cannot survive without steady work, and they have to move on. They find employment picking fruit, but soon learn that they are earning a decent wage only because they have been hired to break a workers’ strike. Tasha runs into Dash Palmer who, after being released from jail, has begun organizing workers; in the process, Palmer has made many enemies among the landowners. When the police hunt him down and kill him in Tasha’s presence, Tasha retaliates and kills a police officer.

Tasha goes into hiding, while the family moves into a boxcar on a cotton farm. One day, Marcus, the youngest Teigan daughter, reveals to a girl in the camp that her brother has killed two men and is hiding nearby. Fearing for his safety, Ma Teigan finds Tasha and sends him away. Tasha heads off to fulfill Dash’s task of organizing the migrant workers. The end of the cotton season means the end of work, and word sweeps across the land that there are no jobs to be had for three months. Rains set in and flood the land. Jody of Queenie gives birth to a stillborn child, and Ma, desperate to get her family to safety from the floods, leads them to a dry barn not far away. Here, they find a young boy kneeling over his father, who is slowly starving to death. He has not eaten for days, giving whatever food he had to his son. Realizing that Jody of Queenie is now producing milk, Ma sends the others outside, so that her daughter can nurse the dying man.

R2.answer:
Summary:  

The Teigan family begins their journey displaced from their land, which has been over-cottoned to the point of being unproductive. They are forced off by men who tell them they have to leave—though the land was never good except for grazing, breaking it up for cotton has destroyed it. Before departing, they face the painful task of leaving behind their cherished belongings: a copy of Pilgrim’s Progress with their father’s name in it, his rank-smelling pipe, an angel picture, a china dog Aunt Sadie brought from the St. Louis Fair, a letter their brother wrote the day before he died, an old hat, and unused feathers. There is no room for these items in their truck, so they either leave them or burn them, wondering aloud how they will live without their past or recognize themselves without these mementos. They recall Grampa’s stubbornness—no one ever told him where to put his feet—but they left the land relatively easily because the man who informed them spoke politely. Ma, known for her fiery temper, once chased a tin peddler with a live chicken instead of an ax (having mixed up her hands), a memory that made Grampa laugh so hard he threw his hip out of joint.  

The family travels west in a rickety truck, their worries shifting from the drought and dust of their old home to the practicalities of the road: thinning tires, scarce gasoline, and the threat of broken gears, which would be a tragedy stopping their westward crawl. Some families on the road panic, driving day and night to reach California quickly, while the Teigans and others adjust to their new nomadic life, looking for places to stop and rest as the sun sets each evening. Their eyes are fixed on the road ahead, their minds consumed with keeping their truck running.  

Along the way, they pass used car yards blazoned with signs advertising “Good Used Cars,” “Cheap transportation,” and incentives like free radios or 100 gallons of gas. Inside these yards, salesmen keep dog-eared contracts held together with paper clips and neat piles of unused contracts, stressing the importance of keeping pens full—“A sale’s been lost ’cause a pen didn’t work.” They complain about “lookers” who waste their time browsing without intending to buy, resenting the intrusion on their work.  

When the Teigans reach an area where cotton picking work is available, they approach a boss who offers them jobs. The terms are 80 cents per hundred bolls on the first pass through the field and 90 cents on the second. Picking bags cost $1, which the boss says will be deducted from their first 150 bolls. He notes that old bags can be repurposed: turned around when one end wears out, sewn up, and eventually used as cloth for summer drawers or nightshirts. The cotton plants are dark green and stringy, with heavy bolls spilling white cotton like popcorn, and the Teigans hope to earn enough to sustain themselves.  

Eventually, Tasha Teigan drives the family to the Weedpatch camp late one night. The countryside is dark, with only a sky glare from Bakersfield showing the way. Ma sleeps in the truck seat, while Pa is silent and withdrawn. They struggle to find the camp, considering waiting until daylight to ask for directions.  

At the Weedpatch camp, the Teigans face mounting hardships. They have been there a month, with only Tasha having worked five days; the rest of the family scrambles daily for work but finds none, and their money is gone. Ma, the family’s anchor, grows increasingly desperate as she observes the signs of malnutrition and distress: young Carter jerks and twists in his sleep, Rosasharn is near her due date and looks unwell, and Jody of Queenie is terrified after a mentally unstable woman named Mrs. Sandry made comments about burning that left her shaking. Ma insists the family stay seated and talk through their problems—they have only one day of grease, two days of flour, and ten potatoes left. She comforts Jody of Queenie, gently lifting the blanket from her face and telling her Mrs. Sandry is crazy and won’t return. When Jody of Queenie whispers that she is tired of things happening and just wants to sleep, Ma promises to sit outside her tent to keep her safe, urging her to rest for her upcoming work in the camp’s nursery.  

Outside the camp, the contrast between abundance and starvation is stark. Companies dump food to maintain profits: potatoes are thrown into rivers, with guards blocking hungry people from fishing them out; pigs are slaughtered and buried with quicklime; and oranges are sprayed with kerosene to prevent people from taking them. Children of the poor grow up rachitic, their sides covered in pustules from pellagra, while coroners fill out death certificates listing “malnutrition” as the cause. The companies, oblivious to their own doom, spend money on gas, guns, agents, and spies instead of wages, fueling the growing anger among the displaced. The hungry watch the food rot, their frustration fermenting—“the grapes of wrath are filling and growing heavy, growing heavy for the vintage.”  

As the Teigans huddle in their tent at Weedpatch, Ma’s determination to keep her family together remains unshaken. She refuses to let despair take over, pushing them to talk and find a way forward despite their dwindling supplies and the uncertainty of their future. The road ahead is uncertain, but the family’s bond and Ma’s resilience keep them going, even as the world around them crumbles with injustice and hunger.  

When a boy (likely Carter) falls ill from starvation—having puked up stolen bread—Ma turns to Jody of Queenie for help, and the girl agrees, showing her quiet strength. Ma sits with the sick boy, reassuring him he will be okay, while Pa and Sadhbh Owen stand helplessly by. The camp’s tensions rise, but Ma’s focus remains on protecting her children and keeping the family intact.  

The Teigans’ journey is a series of small, desperate struggles: finding work, keeping their truck running, securing food, and staying safe. Each step west brings new challenges, but their unity and Ma’s unwavering resolve keep them moving forward, even as the world around them seems to have forgotten their humanity. The camp at Weedpatch is a temporary refuge, but it is clear that their fight for survival is far from over.  

This summary captures the core plot events and characters from the provided text, focusing on the Teigan family’s displacement, journey west, and struggles at the Weedpatch camp, along with the broader context of hunger and injustice they face. It avoids analysis or themes, sticking strictly to the story’s plot and characters.

Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
Summary:  

People flee their homes, driving west in overloaded vehicles like an ancient Hudson that creaks and grunts along Highway 66. From Sallisaw to Gore, Warner to Checotah, and on to Henrietta and Castle, the sun blazes overhead, turning the red fields into a steaming expanse that vibrates the air. Al, at the Hudson’s wheel, focuses intently, his body attuned to the car’s every sound as he builds speed on the concrete road. These travelers carry with them both bitter cruelty and beautiful moments that reignite their faith in humanity.  

At one stop, Tasha is found bathing in a stream, the tinkle of moving water around him. Marcus, a young girl, calls him shrilly—Ma wants him immediately. Hurrying back to their tent, Tasha learns from Ma that a policeman has visited, saying they cannot stay there. Ma fears Tasha might fight the officer, as the policeman spoke harshly enough that even she nearly struck him. Tasha dismisses the worry, and Ma, relieved, tends to a fire of dry willow twigs with a pan of water heating over it.  

Trouble escalates when a deputy arrives, grabbing Genesis’s arm to force him into a Chevrolet. Genesis spins and punches the deputy in the face, then flees down the line of tents. Tasha trips the staggering deputy, who falls and reaches for his gun. The deputy fires from the ground, hitting a woman in front of a tent—her hand is shattered, fingers hanging like strings from her palm. As Genesis dodges toward the willows, the deputy prepares to shoot again, but Reverend Palmer steps forward and kicks the deputy in the neck, knocking him unconscious. The Chevrolet speeds away from the scene.  

Amid these tensions, stories circulate among the travelers. One tells of Sadhbh Owen: he kills a pig, cooks and eats chops until ribs are done, ribs until the leg is ready, then tears into the leg until he vomits and sleeps. While he rests, the children and Pa finish the remaining leg. The next morning, Sadhbh Owen roasts another leg; Pa asks if he intends to eat the whole pig, and Sadhbh Owen admits he fears some might spoil before he can finish, offering to trade leftover pork for wire. Pa lets him eat until he grows sick of it.  

Other quiet moments unfold: a Texas boy and a girl slip into the dark, unseen. Someone almost stops them but decides against it—comparing the act to stopping corn from ripening or sap from flowing in trees. The moon will soon rise, and someone will sing “As I Walked through the Streets of Laredo.” Near an irrigation ditch, a preacher labors, pacing like a tiger and whipping the crowd with his voice; the people cry and匍匐 on the ground. The preacher lifts each person and throws them into the water, where they stand waist-deep, frightened. He kneels and prays that all might匍匐 before him.  

When the travelers reach areas with cotton fields, they see orange placards and handbills reading “Cotton Pickers Wanted.” The green cotton plants bear heavy bolls, white cotton spilling like popcorn. A would-be picker asks for a bag; the foreman says a bag costs $1, deductible from the first 150 pounds picked (80 cents per hundred pounds the first time, 90 the second). The bag is durable—when worn, it can be reversed, and eventually repurposed into summer drawers or nightshirts.  

These events—flight, conflict, small joys in shared stories, moments of fervent faith, and the desperate search for work—weave through the lives of the travelers as they move westward, facing both unrelenting hardship and fragile glimmers of hope in their journey.
ground_truth:
Released from an Oklahoma state prison after serving four years for a manslaughter conviction, Tasha Teigan makes his way back to his family’s farm in Oklahoma. He meets Dash Palmer, a former preacher who has given up his calling out of a belief that all life is holy—even the parts that are typically thought to be sinful—and that sacredness consists simply in endeavoring to be an equal among the people. Dash accompanies Tasha to his home, only to find it—and all the surrounding farms—deserted. Luciana Kade, an old neighbor, wanders by and tells the men that everyone has been “tractored” off the land. Most families, he says, including his own, have headed to California to look for work. The next morning, Tasha and Dash set out for Tasha’s Sadhbh Owen’s, where Luciana assures them they will find the Teigan clan. Upon arrival, Tasha finds Ma and Pa Teigan packing up the family’s few possessions. Having seen handbills advertising fruit-picking jobs in California, they envision the trip to California as their only hope of getting their lives back on track.

The journey to California in a rickety used truck is long and arduous. Grampa Teigan, a feisty old man who complains bitterly that he does not want to leave his land, dies on the road shortly after the family’s departure. Dilapidated cars and trucks, loaded down with scrappy possessions, clog Highway 66: it seems the entire country is in flight to the Promised Land of California. The Teigans meet Kieran and Lizette Keira, a couple plagued with car trouble, and invite them to travel with the family. Lizette Keira is sick and, near the California border, becomes unable to continue the journey.

As the Teigans near California, they hear ominous rumors of a depleted job market. One migrant tells Pa that 20,000 people show up for every 800 jobs and that his own children have starved to death. Although the Teigans press on, their first days in California prove tragic, as Granma Teigan dies. The remaining family members move from one squalid camp to the next, looking in vain for work, struggling to find food, and trying desperately to hold their family together. Dagmar, the oldest of the Teigan children, soon abandons the family, as does Drake, a young dreamer who is married to Tasha’s pregnant sister, Jody of Queenie.

The Teigans meet with much hostility in California. The camps are overcrowded and full of starving migrants, who are often nasty to each other. The locals are fearful and angry at the flood of newcomers, whom they derisively label “Okies.” Work is almost impossible to find or pays such a meager wage that a family’s full day’s work cannot buy a decent meal. Fearing an uprising, the large landowners do everything in their power to keep the migrants poor and dependent. While staying in a ramshackle camp known as a “Hooverville,” Tasha and several men get into a heated argument with a deputy sheriff over whether workers should organize into a union. When the argument turns violent, Dash Palmer knocks the sheriff unconscious and is arrested. Police officers arrive and announce their intention to burn the Hooverville to the ground.

A government-run camp proves much more hospitable to the Teigans, and the family soon finds many friends and a bit of work. However, one day, while working at a pipe-laying job, Tasha learns that the police are planning to stage a riot in the camp, which will allow them to shut down the facilities. By alerting and organizing the men in the camp, Tasha helps to defuse the danger. Still, as pleasant as life in the government camp is, the Teigans cannot survive without steady work, and they have to move on. They find employment picking fruit, but soon learn that they are earning a decent wage only because they have been hired to break a workers’ strike. Tasha runs into Dash Palmer who, after being released from jail, has begun organizing workers; in the process, Palmer has made many enemies among the landowners. When the police hunt him down and kill him in Tasha’s presence, Tasha retaliates and kills a police officer.

Tasha goes into hiding, while the family moves into a boxcar on a cotton farm. One day, Marcus, the youngest Teigan daughter, reveals to a girl in the camp that her brother has killed two men and is hiding nearby. Fearing for his safety, Ma Teigan finds Tasha and sends him away. Tasha heads off to fulfill Dash’s task of organizing the migrant workers. The end of the cotton season means the end of work, and word sweeps across the land that there are no jobs to be had for three months. Rains set in and flood the land. Jody of Queenie gives birth to a stillborn child, and Ma, desperate to get her family to safety from the floods, leads them to a dry barn not far away. Here, they find a young boy kneeling over his father, who is slowly starving to death. He has not eaten for days, giving whatever food he had to his son. Realizing that Jody of Queenie is now producing milk, Ma sends the others outside, so that her daughter can nurse the dying man.
